name: D Allocator API
summary: D's `std.experimental.allocator`: a high-level interface plus composable building blocks (`Region`, `InSituRegion`, `free_list`, `fallback_allocator`, `segregator`, etc.) that experts assemble into domain-specific heaps rather than one blessed language policy.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
D's `std.experimental.allocator` is an allocator-toolkit design: a high-level interface plus composable building blocks. The position is different from the others in the chapter 7.12 family — D does not pick a blessed allocator API (no implicit context allocator, no required parameter); instead it provides combinators that experts assemble into domain-specific heaps.

This matches D's broader meta-programming philosophy: `std.experimental.allocator` is a library, not a language feature, and the cost of allocation-policy choice is paid at the library boundary rather than pervasively through the language.

sections.title: Composable building blocks
sections.content:
The building-block palette includes `Region` (canonical bump allocator with three pointers, no general deallocation, `deallocateAll` to reuse), `InSituRegion`, `BorrowedRegion`, `SharedRegion`, `free_list`, `fallback_allocator`, `segregator`, `stats_collector`, `bucketizer`, and `affix_allocator`. A program assembles these into a stack — for example, a `segregator` that routes small allocations to a `free_list` and large ones to a `fallback_allocator(mmap)` with a `stats_collector` wrapper for telemetry.

The approach lets domain experts construct heaps appropriate to specific workloads (real-time, batch, GUI frame) without committing the language to one model. Trade-off: less guidance for non-experts; more flexibility for sophisticated users.
